pages.text:
On the walk across the moorland we saw stacks of large hay bales, but when we got up close we saw that they had, in fact, baled the local vegetation - bracken + heather. It would make marvellous walls for sheep shelters? Edible?
	Simon had gone out to look at the stars which were v. bright on the friday night, however on the Saturday night the clouds came.
__Sunday__ The night was very cold. We wore hats + socks. I got too hot in the night + took of my jeans + socks + then regretted it.
	We had switched to red gas - propane - but it ran out (the same can that we had had all along) + the blue gas can barely work. Fortunately the heater stayed on while I got dressed. It was cold at breakfast + I couldn't cook toast.
	On the journey we stopped at to look at an engine house in the Brandon Hills. It hauled a cable for a train up to the mines. This was the top of the cable [illegible]. The old [?Gindling] had metal bars sticking out of the floor for

anchoring the cables. There were chimneys showing where furnaces had been for providing steam power. We didn't have time to go down the path + explore where the cable went.
	At 12.50. we arrived at the Thatch, Queens Camel, for lunch with the Urbaincyks. Eve had mis-map read a bit. We had a delicious lunch + the house is very pretty.
__Saturday October 24th.__
Simon + Eve take Margaret + Helen out to tea.

__Friday April 9th 2010__
@ 6.15. Simon + Eve set off with Tonic post letter, reverse, fetch ½ bottle wine + ½ bottle of milk of kitchen counter, 6.20. set off again. £12 ***
	We drove thru [?Sunshine] to __the Walks__ Penrhos, Wales, on the way to the Brecons 8.05. good journey. Lovely plain with

distant hills all round. Daffodils (late spring) 3 graves in middle of camp site, house wi. shutters, pond, bridge, Welsh flag, clean loo.
  __April 10th__. Mummy's + Daddy's 81st wedding anniversary.
  Gorgeous sunshine. Relaxed. On the road by 9.15.
  We parked + had coffee + set off with lunch on a beautiful walk at about 10.45 from Torpantau where the Brecon to Merthyr railway emerged from a tunnel. We followed a forestry road down to the river where we crossed to the west bank and walked north parallel to the river until the Lower Neuadd Reservoir.  We crossed the dam after having lunch in a sunny spot and turned north again up the old "Roman Road" to the pass overlooking Brecon.  Eve thought Tonic had walked enough so carried him back from here in the backpack.  It was a marvellous

walk + we caught some sun.**
  As we neared __Crickhowell__ I telephoned Joan Clarke, now 92, + we went round to see her taking supper.  Briefly, in the hall, we met her niece, Jane, who was on her way out to supper.  Joan seems very happy where she lives Flat 21 Maes y Ffynnon, Beaufort St. 01873-810664.  (campsite £15, expensive + extra charge for showers)

__Sunday April 11th__.
  Another sunny morning.  We ate up half breakfast, walked up to St. Edmund's church where we heard about the re-setting-up of a credit union + then walked down for the other half of breakfast.  Tonic + I also went to see the dog walk by the river.
  10.30. we set off.
  We visited Abbey Dore.  Four young men in one family died in WW2 in the RAF + their 16 year old [?sister] died in 1936, according to one of the [illegible] graves outside.  We had a lunch in the pub carvery where they are polite but the food was pretty dire.
  We called in at Trampley on the way home + had tea in the summer house.

Friday May 7th.

6.25t we left Westfield [?S] & drove to Moreton on the Marsh, Simon had forgotten the map so we didn't find out original campsite near Blockley. We ended up arriving too late (8.10) & slept in carpark [illegible].

__Saturday May 8__th

Cold, miserable weather. By 8.30 we were having breakfast at William's & Bryony's B&B in Lower Swell, Cotswolds. Very fancy B&B, so posh Simon had to repark the van. Old vine in the conservatory.
By 10 we were at __Pangolin Foundry__ in Chalford. Andy, the designer had organised for a group of students to visit & find out about mould-making & casting metal sculptures - hand sized giants (Bull in Bull ring). Alloys - bronze. (Cornley's are cast in iron, industrially, so he doesn't use them) lots of work for [?Admira] [?Hirst]. 92 people work there. Very interesting. Lost wax. Sand moulds [?or] with resin, injected with CO₂. Sue, the designer, asked lots of questions.
     We had lunch in the van, then rested. At 4.40, W&B picked us up in their super car & drove us to Winchcombe to David Thistlethwaite's

& Sarah Loveday's exhibition of paintings. It was good to see David & Ali after so many years.
     We went out to supper at the Fox, lovely walnut & honey tart. Then back to our campsite, __Folly Farm__, **** £12 where we're parked under beautiful apple blossom.

__Sunday May 9__th

     Slightly better weather today. We again breakfasted with William & Bryony. The landlady's husband had been an architect for townhalls. We think he bought the old rectory farm & then found the foundations of out buildings & managed to build about 3 more Cotswold houses in the [?old] garden: Glebe House, Barn Cottage etc.
     We set off to Snowshill Manor, having a bit of trouble with a cycle race enroute. The garden to Snowshill Manor is the most lovely I've seen, on the side of a hill, compartments. Benches, ponds, barns. Model village, old fire engines. Charles Wade's collection of 22,000 objects grew so large that he stored it in [?&] the Manor house & moved, with his wife, into the priest's

captions: Snows-hill Manor Glouos [?Selt] South Front
pages.text:
[photo of Snowshill Manor, south front]

Snows-
hill
Manor

Glouos

[?Selt]
South
Front

house behind it, where he installed electricity &
hot water. He often sat there next to the fire
in a hall porter's chair. A rope was next to

him & when he pulled it, it worked the
bellows in the next room. Bryony & I enjoyed
looking at the dolls' houses. Simon &
looked at the warping? machine, large,
6ft high, 6ft diametre, for # wrapping
silk thread around at the correct tension
for the weaver. Lots of spinning wheels.
   Wagons from different countries, ships,
clocks, walking frames for toddlers, one
for round a post (on a ship?) Crafts,
colours, useful, beautiful things. One of
the best places I've been.
   We had lunch in the carpark. They
drove back to London & we are going
back to Birmingham.
   __23 May Sunday__
   Traffic jams as we left [?Sham] at 1.30
to take Margaret & Helen out for a
Strawberry Tea. A lovely sunny
afternoon.

captions: Music room, Snowshill Manor. There is also a child's harp, drums, recorder + a Serpent, covered in leather.
pages.text: Music room, Snowshill Manor. There is also a child's harp, drums, recorder + a Serpent, covered in leather.

Friday June 11th.
	At 2.15. we left for Wales. Simon, Eve, Peter, Magda Lipińska. And Tonic.
	We drove to Ludlow. There were pigeons nesting next to the loos in the Castle. They were setting up a stage for Othello. Then, after much trouble, we found a bar serving tea. The tea shops shut at 4.45!
	Charles is also with us in the blue car. He found the way to the campsite + telephoned us when we took the wrong turning on the way to the campsite. *** 2 loos + 2 showers.
	__Elstich Barns__ campsite -
showers.
	There's a covered road off it where Peter + Magda walked, but supper wasn't ready when they [strikethrough: out] got back at [strikethrough: 8] 7.30. We drank Magda's present - Zubrovka made out of bison grass. Delicious. Deceptively easy to drink! Lovely local straw berries.
	The farmer came to visit, bringing an enormous old wasps' nest in a 'black sack' that he'd just removed from his roof. Lovely views.

pages.sketch_desc: Pencil sketch at top of page showing a landscape view of rolling hills/mountains, with some tree/vegetation outlines, labelled as the view from the campsite at Eastward, Shropshire.
pages.text:
Saturday June 12th

View from table + chairs behind hedge at camp site Eastward, Shropshire.
we need to buy, bread milk tea glue cafetiere

    We went to Stokesay Castle + each listened to a tape recording of its history as we wandered around. A beautiful place. It surrendered to Cromwell's troops in the civil war. Lawrence of Ludlow had permission to build a castle from the king. It wasn't one of the Marches Castles.
Enroute to the Elan Valley Reservoirs we had an outdoor lunch at a pub. There were 3 or more Reservoirs, built 1886-19[illegible]. At any time they had 5,000 men working on them, 12,000 men were on the books. A final reservoir was opened by Queen Elizabeth, her first appointment on ascending

captions: Stokesay Castle 1280 fortified manor house
pages.text:
Stokesay Castle
1280 fortified
manor house

s the throne 1954. It must have been an amazing feat to feed everyone – the valleys have very, very few houses. The lakes are all long & thin. Cwmiswyth*** east of Devil's Bridge
    We've come to a beautiful campsite in a narrow valley. Peter & Magda have gone off for a walk. We are next to running water & there are cows on the flat valley bottom.
    It looked & sounded really beautiful, but as it got dark, 1000s midges came out to torment us: I opened the windows in the middle of the night, but [illegible] in the morning the midges were back & Simon had to open the back to change the gas (red to blue) & they all came in again.
__Sunday June 13th.__
    Although Peter found some news of the GB/US football match for the World Cup, it wasn't until we got to a garage & asked that we found out it was a 1–1 draw. We found a church service, Matins in Welsh, warm welcome, at Tregaron. Nice craft shop.

Rain. We went for a drink at the pub [illegible] set off & had lunch in the van. We would have liked to walk. Instead we stopped for a cup of tea, dropping off Peter & Magda at Lyttleton Rd on the way so that Magda could meet Thomas & family.

__Saturday July 4th__
    Simon & Eve & Tonic went over to Trimpley. Simon borrowed a wheelchair from church & he pushed Margaret round Arley Arboretum while Helen held Tonic. Then we had tea in the van.

__Saturday July 17th.__
    10 Simon & Eve set off, dropping Tonic at [?Karsels]. We are driving to [?Thornesweathers] for Momiji's & Koroku's graduation party. On the A14 in the other direction was a huge jam. After Cambridge we went on a parallel road for lunch at Burwell (then Swaffham & [?leds]) before rejoining the A12.
    I swum with John [&] [illegible]
    Most of the Haines family were at Sea [illegible] & the Nelsons & Koroku's friends & Momiji's friends

We had champagne & smoked salmon at Seamark + the Club House gave us a very good meal. Country-dancing followed, + sending off fire lanterns into the sky. John had been spending the last week in a tent, during Maxine's + David's second week after my Headlands let.

	The young danced in a disco in the garage. Jane & Harry (2 crushed vertebrae) slept at the Dolphin while we turned in to the van on Annie's Cottage drive. She had Kate, Jamie, Louisa, Klara & her girls sleeping at the house.
__Sunday July 18th__
Sally joined us at breakfast/coffee in the garden. Before that, I joined Annie in a swim in the sea. After that we drove up to Seamark + said goodbye to John who is off to Sweden tomorrow.
We went over the Dartford Bridge &, having left [?Thorpeness] at 10, managed to get 1.42 [?train] instead of 2.40, eating lunch during the crossing. On past Dunkirk to near Ghent, difficult road

signs - motorway exit nos. are separately signed. Most annoying poor signposting of campsite - only one tiny sign to Camping Greenpark (Groenpark)**/* at a tiny roundabout. we went back & forth near Dendermonde + passed it at least once. The receptionist said they've not allowed to advertise.
	The loo/washroom is a plastic portacabin on a permanent base with an enormous solar panel - partition 2ft above floor she shelters loo. Cosy & clean but only 2 wide [?locks].
We walked out to the lake for a delicious meal at Mistral, charming shy waitress.
__Saturday__
	Cheek-by-jowl campsite but polite people. Donk = sandy dunes in marshy land. We drive to Temse. The parking machine swallows a euro so we get a free ticket & dash to look at the river - muddy. ¼ mile metal bridge, longest in B. Use of land-peat → rushes for baskets →poplar for matches.
	On then West → St. Amands. Poor signposting. The signpost at a cross roads will point to 10 different restaurants, but not give the names of villages.

"Stand by Dame Anne"
"She was bloody marvellous"

Friday 14th October 2011
We set off, Simon, Eve + Tonic (13) to visit Grandma in the Shruberg at Kidderminster. She agreed to move to Sunrise in Edgbaston. We had a glass of wine.
    We set off for the farm. On the way Jeremy telephoned to say that Tatana had died that morning. It's very sad.
    Thomas has started a teacher training course. William is waiting to get liscensed for Samphire. Peter is doing films + has bought an ambassador. John is in the Gambia for 2 weeks in his 2nd year at Swansea + he's bought a glorious red BMA. Charles has just started at Imperial – innovation + entrepreneurship.
    We arrived at the farm + Kate gave us a delicious supper – 4 little beef wellingtons, roast potatoes, beetroot, carrots + parsnips + blackberry + apple crumble. It was very cold in the night.

__BROOKHOUSE FARM__

£80,000 per yr.

Grapes
3000 bottles per year

Apples
800 tons

Muller - ¼  Solaris ¾ acre
Cabinet ¾
2 or 3 bottles per vine  3000 litres
3 tons grapes
19 tons per acre
40 or 60 acres

[map annotation:] Toby &
Ali[?ce]

VINEYARD [arrow pointing to vineyard rows on map]

Jon's [?dome] [?done]

Mike's workshop.

__Saturday 15th October__
      We breakfasted at 8.. Jamie cooked us bacon. Jamie took us round the farm. He took us first to Simon's surprise - a vineyard! White grapes. Eventually 3 tons of juice a year!
      We also saw Mike's workshop - lovely cabins + Josh's dome + Toby + Ali's chestnut plantings barn. Crunchie was leading out the 2 horses to drag wood.
      After a cup of coffee we drove south to Wilsham House B + B, where I am hoping to celebrate my 60th birthday in July 2013. Richard + Jane welcomed us, showed us the farm (which has [?4] double bedrooms) explained about the B + B across the drive (which we couldn't see because it has people in) answered our questions + made suggestions. It's absolutely beautiful.
      Then we drove on past Lorna Doone's Farm to a nearby camp site, near __Oare__ ★ ★ ★
      Lots of girls doing D of E?
      On [the] to Barnstaple. Simon slept from 9 pm - 8 am, his ear + throat hurt + he's very tired, but better after a good sleep. It

captions: Appledore from Instow Quay, North Devon
View from shop where we bought the cart
pages.text:
was warmer in the night.
      __Sunday__ On to Barnstaple where we managed to buy Micron + cycled along the Tarka Trail (Barnstaple → Instow Rd?)
[postcard: Appledore from Instow Quay, North Devon]
View from shop where we bought the cart

just over 12 miles. lovely country side. We passed salt meadows along the estuary. There only flood at very high tides. They're useful for bird life & growing salt lamb when sheep are grazed in the summer. just like Baie de Somme.
   Back to the van & we drove west to near Padstow where the food is supposed to be good. I've just had an unpleasant shower (steeply raked floor, soapy blocked drain, only one hook) to find Simon struggling to light any gas after charging the cylinder. It will be __very__ cold tonight without even a fridge. I got very chilled with all the doors open. No lighting success on anything. We finally walked up to the pub which doesn't serve food on a Sunday. So I had a gin & tonic & Simon had a beer. The people at the next table were nice - Cradley Heath. Simon managed to light the kettle for tea. Then other things lit. Maybe the cylinder was just too full of gas. I slept in my clothes.

captions: Rick Stein's Fish & Chips
pages.text:
We drove to Padstow & caught the ferry across & ambled round the pretty town. There were a nice family on the ferry. The young & their child lived in Vienna, the Grandmother in little Witley. (Her brother has 2 acres of vines & a wedding barn at Shrawley.)
   Lots of tat in the shops but some nice things too. Lots of restaurants owned by Rick Stein. We had fish & chips. Good fast service. Light batter. Plaice over-cooked. Not much better than Aldeburgh but Simon said he didn't feel as ill as usual after f+c

We timed the ferry just right + drove on the the lost Gardens of Heligan. Very pretty £10 entry. Lovely jungly valley. OLd[?fad] about it. Peaceful inshpite of all the people.     BlagyGate    *+*
   On to __Doubletrees Campsite St. Austell__
V. windy. Lovely view. Simon has changed the windscreen wipers + light bulb. The loos + bath showers are good.

__Tuesday__
We reached the Eden Project by 10. We enjoyed it - names of bee plants, renewable fuel - coppiced birch, hazel, willow in small power stations or heating. We had a Mediterranean meal - Portugese stew with mussels - cayenne - best meal of the holiday. The rain forest atmosphere + pepper were good for Simon's nose. The van was parked in the dog shelter for Tonic.
 We returned to the campsite + had a late walk down to the local church. We have been extraordinarily lucky with the rain - mainly at

night, pinging on the metal roof + a couple of sprinkles in the day.
   __Wednesday October 19th__
 We left the lovely campsite at 9.30 for home. Elevenses was at Roadford Lake, a modern reservoir, to feed all the west country. We could have cycled (around) it. A nature reserve in the north near
      We had a good cheese + apple lunch on a field at Puriton. A farmer had put lots of pvcell panels horizontally in a field.
      We have brought home a banana plant, a tea plant + some [?leg] touch-sensitive grass.

[Printed Eden Project leaflet, no handwritten text. Aerial photograph at top showing the Eden Project site with printed labels: "Rainforest Biome", "Covered Biome Entr[ance]", "Mediterranean Bio[me]", "Stage", "Link", "Land train", "Outdoor Biome". Below, printed sections:

__Stage__
The home of our seasonal events programme:
- Den building
- Ice skating
- Concerts (Eden Sessions).

__Outdoor Biome__
Ten years ago this Biome (with the sky for a roof) was a barren landscape, with no soil and no plants. Today find crops that provide your foods, fuels, medicines and materials. Explore our wild places too and consider why we need to conserve them.

__Rainforest Biome__
The largest rainforest in captivity. Climb to the top on the Rainforest Lookout, find out how rainforests keep us alive and how to help conserve them. This Biome is also about trade and connections: find a smallholding which grows cocoa for chocolate, compare the Malaysian back garden to your own. These places are far away but we're connected.

__Link__
(covered Biome [entrance])
This grass-roofed [building] is the entrance [to both] covered Biomes [—] it helps keep in [heat]. You'll find loos, [a] membership de[sk], delicious food [at] Eden Bakery.]

[Printed Eden Project leaflet continued, no handwritten text. Top: aerial photograph continuation with printed labels: "Lift and Bridge", "Core", "Visitor Centre", "[Ra]inforest Biome Entrance", "[Med]iterranean Biome". Left column contains partial text from the cut-off Link and Visitor Centre sections (continuation from L page). Centre column:

__Mediterranean Biome__
Sights, scents and stories from the Mediterranean, South Africa and California. Discover [text continues beyond cut]

[Printed numbered location list in a brown/tan box:]

__Rainforest Biome__
1 Tropical Islands
2 West Africa
3 Tropical South America
4 Rainforest Lookout

__Mediterranean Biome__
5 The Mediterranean Basin
6 South Africa
7 California
8 Citrus Grove
9 Mediterranean Cafe

__Outdoor Biome__
10 The Bee
11 The Theatre

13 Wild Cornwall
14 Grow Your Own Allotments

16 The Spiral Garden
17 The Garden
18 Plants for Fuel
19 Myth and Folklore
20 Tea Ship
21 Land train
22 Flowerless Garden
23 Crops
24 The Orchard
25 Beer and Brewing
26 Metal Giant
27 Plants for a changing climate
28 Metals, minerals and mine[s]
29 Plants for health
30 Hemp
31 WEEE man

__The Core__
31 Jo's Cafe
32 The Nutcracker
33 Seed

__The Visitor Centre__
34 Shop
35 Plant Deck
36 Apple Cafe
37 Driftwood horse

__The Link__
38 The Eden Bakery

Right column:
__Core__
Our education, arts and events hub. Home to 'the Seed' our massive [sculp]ture, our schools [prog]rammes and a range [of exh]ibitions. Try out th[e] [world]'s largest nutcracke[r] [and] 'services' that [we] provide (includ[ing] [climate] control) and [disco]ver some of [the] [many] ways in which [com]munities can adapt [to] the needs of the 21st century. Delicious food in Jo's Cafe too.]

captions: The Mawddach Estuary looking towards Barmouth
pages.text:
Friday April 27th 2012.
     Simon, Eve & Tinder (6 mo) set off at 6 for Barmouth area, the only place in the country where rain is not
[Photo caption, written vertically along right edge of photo:] The Mawddach Estuary looking towards Barmouth

captions: The Mawddach Estuary & the Rhinogydd
pages.text:
forecast. Cold & windy, fantastic view of Estuary from Craig Wen campsite LL39 1BQ. Very difficult entry - we had to turn round twice
[Photo caption, written vertically along left edge of photo:] The Mawddach Estuary & the Rhinogydd

In the morning we managed to harness Tinder into) Eve's Brompton saddle handlebar bag & go on his first bike ride. We ran about 6 of the 16 miles. Simon had him on a lead when he ran. Gorgeous scenery. Not many people. We looked around Dolgellau & had a good lasagna lunch. Blues & greys & green & brown & yellow. It was hard work cycling up the Estuary against the wind, but we were blown home. It was the Mawddach Bike Trail - a disused railway. The last bit up to the campsite was an exhausting 20 minute climb with the bikes. Tinder yapped at sheep while crossing the field.
    Poorer weather later in the day. We walked up to look at 2 yurt + a catamaran in the woods. A group from Mochant[leth]

captions: plum jam label from Dolgellau
MADE IN WALES / £3.15 / Cynhyrchwyd yng Nghymru
pages.text:
[plum jam label from Dolgellau]

were doing a survey - how to make the camp fire shore communal. I suggested a long fire, for people to look out across the Estuary.
    We moved over to a better pitch (£2 extra) overlooking the sea more. Glorious view. We had hoped for another walk in the morning, but it was windy & rainy[so]

so we headed for home. On the way we stopped at Wroxeter, (London, Cirencester, St Albans) 4th largest Roman town, 5,000 people & enjoyed the museum & reconstruction of a Roman town house.
June 9th
    8.45. Simon, Eve & Tinder are off to Annette's birthday party. Harry died yesterday. Annette (8th) John (May 31st) Max (12th) & Eve (6th) all have birthdays. Lizzie is visiting from Australia. Max, Tania, Kate, Dan, Annette, Lizzie, Pamela & John will be there. Max is about to move to Singapore. Tania's having another baby. The weather has been horribly cold & rainy for weeks, except when we were in Granada.
    The weather has held good. Warmish sun & cloud. Benjie 5/6 Franklin 3 (Max's) & Toby 3, Isadora 2 (Kate's) were here too, all good children. We had a super meal. Visited playground, evening concert at Wingfield church, old re-constructed organ, near Halesworth, Amici sang. Enthusiastic conductor. We slept on Annette's drive. Max in house, Kate

in hotel. Lizzie has been here 6 days.
Sunday we sat in garden sunshine then had hot dogs at Euston Agricultural Show. Tinder yapped, big & little steam engines & a steam bus. 2.45. We head for home. A good week end.

__About June 16th__
    Simon, Eve & Tinder went on a day trip to Pershore. Simon bought a battery for the van. We wandered around Pershore & bought a mirror for dressing up in the play room.
    The Abbey is beautiful. They allowed us to take Tinder in. Another man came in with his dog & it weed in the church.
    It was a wet day but we enjoyed it.

Sat. July 21st (The day after our 38th Wedding Anniversary) Simon + Eve set off at 4 pm for Italy. We called in at Chad for Simon to set up the ansaphone with instructions for the holiday. We are listening to the radio - Tour de France ends in Paris tomorrow. John has texted from Kent, on his way to Paris now, that he has had his offer - 272,000 accepted on the Mumbles House. Very happy. Charles is house hunting to rent within 40 minutes of Warwick. Then he's going on holiday to Germany + meeting Jenny. Peter is filming in the desert outside Urumchi. William + Anjony are staying in Somerset then taking Tess to see the farm. Thomas + Naomi are taking the children to France. John will be doing physiological testing at high altitude in Chile.
8 We arrived in __Stallisfield__ again, the same as last year. The pub runs a casino in huts across the road. A nice little campsite. We went for a walk. Simon has had a long day 7 haircut with Toni, 8.30 set of to pick up Helen + take her to

Margaret at Sunrise. Mowed all the lawns. 3 picked up Helen + sent her home in a taxi. A relief to be here.
__Sunday July 22nd.__
	Went for bike ride from Charing. Very pretty village shops. We missed 9.30 church service, invited to coffee before 11 youth service in barn. Friendly. Left before service. Queues at Channel tunnel terminus. Irate English woman from Dutch car at foreign bus. The wait was long + stressful + during the trip the van (a small VW) shunted into the back of us. We pushed it away + no damage done. 1 hour late.
	We have stopped for tea at an Aire near lille. A man is cooking on a blue gas bottle outside behind the cab, a flame right next to the gas tank. He's in a [?] [illegible] Czech lorry. There are also Czech lorries + a row of identical Portugese lorries with holy mascots.
	It took ages to find the Belgican campsite - lots of windy roads up a hill to St. Seveur. Finally we got there, only to find that it had

been closed – Te Coop – for sale. We ate supper on the road outside (8.15) & then walked over to look at the magnificent view from the restaurant carpark (also Te Coop) So we moved the van & had the best view ever. By night, with the lights, the valley & hillside looked much more built-up than by day.
	Oh di! clicking in the night. The fridge has run out of gas, so I switch it off.

__Monday__
	Oh woe is me! For the first time in 23 years we have run out of gas – other tub empty too. (Norway doesn't count as we couldn't buy gas there). So no tea, coffee or toast & Simon shaved in cold water. We leave earlier than normal, 6 hours to get to Christina.
	We bought bread from a wood burning oven. The motorway after the nice Belgian fields was very boring. We had lunch soon after turning the corner near Cologne.
	At Christina's Anna Katarina met us at the gate. Christina & Victoria were also

at home, but Michael & Eric[?12] & Constance were at Eric's school b.b.q. They arrived & we had a b.b.q supper in the beautiful garden. VW well-behaved children & Michael & Christina made us feel most welcome. The back is off the house. Michael is putting in insulation & will then replaster. Scaffolding. 2 of the 5 bedrooms are also under construction. We slept downstairs in the parents' bedroom. When the babies were little Michael looked after them until 2am & Christina [illegible] 2 am, unless all 3 cried at once. It must have been exhausting.
	On [?Wednesday] ~~Wednesday~~ Tuesday we went shopping with Christina for gas (& looked at camping vans, & food for a picnic. Michael took the afternoon off. Christina & I cycled to meet the children at school (who go to & fro on scooters). I saw the impressive after school club – a bistro for cooking, a sewing machine, a work bench. Christina said at first she was against

captions: Münster zu Ulm S[?]. Höchste Kirche der Welt [?] Meter hoch.
ULM an der Donau

pages.text:
it, but the children really enjoy it.
It's run by nice older women & the
children meet up with friends.
School is 8-12. They can stay at
the club 12-2 or 12-5.
After lunch we rested (v. hot) & then
went on a bike ride. Ferry - taking
bikes + 5 cars. Park. The children
played clock golf. Hot sun. R. [?Weckar]
Picnic. Chat - lovely. We saw village
Eric cycled home [?alone] [?hands]
the rest of us missed the ferry &
cycled over the bridge.
Wed [Thursday]. July 25th. The chil's school is over but
they have to go to church today,
which they don't want. Then holidays
until Sept 7th.
We left at 8.20. down motorway.
Traffic getting out of Ulm was awful
V. bad road signs. We enjoyed looking

at Ulm. Jewish window, Goose poking gargoyle, choir carvings, central altar. American Meier designed modern town hall. Difficult lunch - making decisions in a foreign language.
	We drove on through lovely grassy fields of Bavaria to __Ellessee__ an old campsite on a lake.
	Aitrang / Allgau
	It's well laid out with good clean showers. You have to pay to swim in the lake. Art classes - arty things round campsite, Metal work, glass, poems! Lights in showers painted. Clean air. Enormous stones marking levels of pitches. What was there originally? Fresh bread at breakfast. It rained in the evening.
	~~Friday~~ Thursday -
	We pass dinky red trains. Bavaria is very beautiful. Hot terrible traffic jams caused by trivial roadworks. German motorway, too dirty. Late morning we pass thro Austria. Past Innsbruck. on to Bolsano. A long day's driving. We keep pink Dolomite Mountains on our left. Fruit trees. V. fertile valley. Castle.
	Lake Garda comes in sight & we join the

captions: tr@ns.it / www.areatransit.it — photo of campsite/transit area on lake shore
pages.text:
queue of traffic down the hill. We try 4 different campsites, all full. In the end we go to [?Bolsari] Transit which we think will be just a lay by but instead it turns out to be a Check-in/post site just for camping vans. Amazing. No lights at night. Only the van width next to us for table & chairs. Sat nice people. The van opposite have a kid russel + border terrier. They give us their fresh left-over bread for our supper. The woman next to us has a 10 year old son, Felix. I've never seen a single parent woman camping before, on her own. She's from Dresden. She teaches circus, she [illegible] in Sheffield + London.
	She + son with [illegible]
	[?Gloucester]
	[?Winchsea]
	[illegible]
	[illegible]
	[sticker: tr@ns.it / www.areatransit.it — photo of campsite/transit area on lake shore]
	Friday we [?sat] after breakfast. V. beautiful.

captions: Verona Opera
pages.text:
off in the van left at Garda, [?Affri], back road to N. __Verona__. Difficult to find way. Van GPS slow. We come up a road marked ~~metres~~ 2.1m. The van is 2m. So we had
5cm spare
The van only just scrapes thro. I was anxious 3 roads before the end. Simon only got anxious at the very end. Ivy.
__Camping Castel San Pietro__ * * * * Incredibly hot. We have a shower, eat + [?fix] Simon joins queue for a tent pitch. We have to leave the van on the road. Next to motor cyclists who smoke out on the terraces.
     It's part of a [?B] castle with lots of terraces, higgledy piggledy.
                               Verona Opera

captions: Stage for Carmen
pages.text:
The motor cyclists turn out to be really nice. we give them tea + rock cakes on the terrace. Then we set off down the hill, after a second shower. We visit the cathedral + see Romanesque carvings, a few. We sit outside + watch very
[?Stage] for Carmen
[photo]
posh people dressed to the Nines, going to the opera. Wonderful style + clothes. One very fat lady is dressed in a plain white linen dress + you can see all her fat quivering as she moves. A good supper. Cold veg soup. Croutons with little dollops of cream [?cheese]! Then reglets cooked in myrtle! Delicious but rich.

captions: MUSEO DI CASTELVECCHIO
pages.text:
The opera was incredible. 9.15 – 1.05. 4 Acts + 20 minute intervals. There were 6 horses + 3 donkeys. A very good Carmen. The tenor was a

[postcard: panoramic view of Verona with river; ticket stub reads: ROBERT CAPA / Biglietto: Intero / Prezzo: / Valido il 29/07/2012 Emesso il 28/07/2012 alle 19.21 / Comune di Verona Cultura]

substitute. It was horribly hot + sticky. People kept being ill. The very nice, unsophisticated Russian woman next to me started for to drink water + fan abt, gave a groan + passed out. Men with bright

[postcard: aerial view of Museo di Castelvecchio, Verona; Comune di Verona Assessorato alla Cultura]

orange trousers dashed around but it took them ages to reach us. Russia came round after we had splashed lots of water on her after about 5 minutes. She looked ghastly, pale, with owl eyes + running mascara. A nearby woman said we shd. get her lying down with her feet up, but the seating was so steep that we didn't dare move her. She refused to leave with the orange men. But after one more act they left. Then another woman 2 rows behind fainted. Unbearably hot until 3rd act. Walking home, up the long hill to the campsite, was exhausting + I didn't feel well with the heat.

    The [the] German motorcyclists only got back after us because he got lost. He's just completed his pilot's training. I hope he flies better than he walks.

Saturday Too hot in the van at breakfast. Too hot every minute of the day + evening. So we are missing Palladio in N. Italy + heading for the Alps near France. 5 minutes after a shower we pour sweat again.

    We walk down into the town [Verona] + see the

lovely squares, 2 squares. A whale bone is suspended in the passage between them. It will fall when a person walks thro whose never told a lie. We also go to see a wonderful Robert Capa exhibition & the city museum with beautiful paintings.

     The town is very, very attractive. The houses have [sketch of window frame] wide stone frames around their windows [sketch of window frame] & the planners have got shopowners to put wide stone frames around the plate glass windows, too. The kerbs of the pavements are often flush with the road.

     We catch a taxi back to the campsite & buy the excellent yoghurt & Simon brings us lunch on the lower terrace. We nap & I wash clothes. While I wash, a Dutch woman, who's come to refill her water bottle, stops to chat & it made clothes washing very enjoyable to just chat while I did it....

     R. Whelan wrote about Capa "Capa declared he was willing to risk his life to cover only those wars in which he loved one side & hated the other. Steinbeck: "He could show the horror of a whole people in the face of a child."

     We are hot in the night : sweat streaming.

Sunday 6.40. wake up. Get up even with tea to avoid ghastly temperature. Temperature OK until after breakfast. We take down tent & drive to St. Zeno. Lovely pink stones & red & white stripes. Carvings! [?Verotgiano]. [?Mantegna].

     (There were fossils in the pink steps at the campsite.)

     A beautiful [?durate] cloister with delicate columns & a loggia in it. Welcoming.

     We found a good, cheap supermarket; & bought lots of food, having almost run out. We drive WNW.

     [Lago] Orta   [Lago] Maggiore   [Lago] Como         [Lago] Garda

are the lakes of N. Italy. Lake Orta is the smallest & least known, only 13K top to bottom. There's a peninsula with a village called Orto San Giulio & the Island at the end is called Isola San Giulio. It's tiny & covered in buildings & has lots of [crossed out word] nuns, in enclosed order, average age 40. The village & surrounding hill sides are beautiful. We are lucky to get a [?site] in Camp Cusio [?*****] owned by a family whose [?father] [?niche] inherited the farm

although his children grew up in Peru. They are still developing the site. There are big static tents + log cabins, a pool, a cafe. Very welcoming, too. Lots of walks. Perfect temperature. Lots of Dutch + some Italians. No Germans or other Brits?

  We walk along the dangerous road to the beach + swim.
  In the night I storm out of bed + ask our neighbour to keep quiet. He's chatting loudly on his mob at 1.45. am in his van + the sound's funnelled out by his awning. Finally I slide his van door shut.
  __Monday__ I do a wash, or rather it's done for me for 4 Euros + we walk over the peninsula + look round the village /town + walk back along the coastal path. Lots of fierce dog signs - the rich protecting their property. It's too hot at the end. Lunch in the shade, peaceful afternoon reading. Another trek to the pleasant beach.

captions: Isola San Julio
pages.text:
In the evening we have spaghetti at the café + amaretti biscuits back at the van.
  __Tuesday__
  I wash more clothes. We read in the morning. In the afternoon we climb the hill on the penninsula + admired the view out [to]
[photographs]
the island + of surrounding hills.
  We also wandered around the 20 chapels dedicated to the story of St. Francis. The chapels were built from about 1590-
to 1790. They have frescoed walls & posture figures, life size. The chapels are simple & beautiful & set in the grounds in a pleasing way. It's calm

captions: Città di Domodossola / Assessorato alla Cultura / Apre i suoi Musei
pages.text:
Afterwards we walked down to the village & had [?pnjjos] in the square.

__Wednesday__
We spend the morning in camp. My tummy's bad, so in the afternoon Simon goes off on a train trip on his own.

I travelled 47 km north from St. [?Gialgio] to Domodossola which had an old centre dating from the Middle Ages. The streets still showed the pattern of its five walls.

It acquired a new life when Napoleon chose the Simplon pass as a main route from France to Italy, and yet again [decoration] with the building [of]
